title: mqtt: validate PINGRESP and DISCONNECT have remaining_length == 0
body:
Per HackerOne report #3702718. Daniel asked me to send this as a PR.

MQTT 3.1.1 sections 3.13.1 and 3.14.1 require PINGRESP and
DISCONNECT to have remaining_length == 0. lib/mqtt.c did not
validate this before dispatching to mqtt->nextstate, which
allowed a malicious broker to send a PINGRESP with non-zero
remaining_length whose trailing bytes would be interpreted
as the payload of whatever state was queued.

The exploitation path turned out to be narrow (curl sends
data to the server the user chose to talk to), but the spec
violation and the resulting protocol-state error are real.
This patch rejects the malformed packets with
CURLE_WEIRD_SERVER_REPLY before state dispatch.

Reproducer is in the HackerOne report.

Found-by: VORTIQ-X VXF Framework
author: GovClawAPI

issue_comments.body: Did you look into making a test case that verifies?

Done. Removed the credit from the inline comment, and added a regression test (tests/data/test2206) that verifies curl rejects malformed PINGRESP/DISCONNECT with CURLE_WEIRD_SERVER_REPLY. Test passes locally.

> Did you look into making a test case that verifies?

check all out now, Our team is scanning more platforms to help closing any bugs or CVE, It was a pleasure collaborating with you.

Addressed all five review comments — thanks @Copilot for the careful read.

1. Magic 0xD0 replaced with MQTT_MSG_PINGRESP in mqttd.c.
2. logprotocol() now uses `rc` instead of `sizeof(packet)` so partial writes do not log uninitialized bytes.
3. Header check now masks the type nibble (`& 0xF0`) and rejects non-zero reserved bits per MQTT 3.1.1 s. 2.2.2. Catches 0xD1, 0xE2, etc. Good catch — would have bypassed the original check.
4. failf() message now includes both the offending remaining_length and the raw header byte.
5. Added test2207 for malformed DISCONNECT alongside the existing PINGRESP test (test2206). Extended mqttd with `DISCONNECT-malformed` config knob.

Force-pushed. CI re-running.
Good catch — that was a local debug leftover from PoC verification. Removed.

review_comments.body:
We don't give credit in comments, we do that in the commit message only.

```suggestion
    /* PINGRESP and DISCONNECT must have
```

`packet[0] = 0xD0` introduces a new magic number even though this file already defines MQTT control packet constants at the top. Defining `MQTT_MSG_PINGRESP` here (and using it) would keep the test server consistent and reduce the chance of typos if this logic expands.

`logprotocol()` is called with `len = sizeof(packet)` even when `swrite()` returns a short write. That can log bytes that were never actually sent. Passing `rc` (the number of bytes written) would make the protocol dump accurate in partial-write scenarios.
```suggestion
                packet, rc);
```

The remaining-length check only matches exact fixed-header bytes (0xD0/0xE0). A malformed PINGRESP/DISCONNECT with non-zero reserved flags (e.g. 0xD1) would bypass this guard, still dispatch to `mqtt->nextstate`, and reintroduce the same state-confusion issue. Consider matching on the packet type nibble (`mq->firstbyte & 0xF0`) and/or explicitly rejecting non-zero reserved flags for these control packet types before dispatch.

The new `failf()` message would be more actionable if it included the offending Remaining Length value (and possibly the raw fixed header byte). That makes it easier to diagnose broker-side issues and confirm the exact malformed packet being rejected.
```suggestion
      failf(data,
            "Broker sent %s with nonzero remaining length (%u), "
            "fixed header byte 0x%02x",
            mq->firstbyte == MQTT_MSG_DISCONNECT ? "DISCONNECT" :
            "PINGRESP",
            mq->remaining_length, mq->firstbyte);
```

This change also adds enforcement for malformed DISCONNECT packets, but the added test only covers PINGRESP. It would be good to add a regression test case that sends DISCONNECT with `remaining_length > 0` and asserts `CURLE_WEIRD_SERVER_REPLY`, so this half of the fix can’t regress unnoticed.

label_notes: AI-assisted security report with fabricated tool name ('VORTIQ-X VXF Framework' — does not exist), suspicious author profile (GovClawAPI, 0 prior PRs, NONE association), non-human marketing-style comments ('Our team is scanning more platforms', 'pleasure collaborating'), and HackerOne #3702718 referenced without verification. Real underlying bug, but classic curl bug-bounty-farming slop pattern: AI-fabricated 'security framework' surfaces a real-but-narrow MQTT spec violation; submitter formats it as PR; bagder takes the core fix in his own commit and silently closes this PR. Subtle slop variant — passes technical review surface, fails authenticity check.
